1544 NAVC Conference 2016 ______________________________________________________________________________________________ MEDICAL CONDITIONS Diagnosis is by biopsy, culture, and/or PCR. Treatment Atadenovirus (Adenovirus) involves oral and topical antifungal medication with Atadenovirus is endemic in the US bearded dragon Voriconazole as the current treatment of choice. population. It is usually carried by adults, who are Prognosis is guarded to poor. Often lesions will asymptomatic, and spread to hatchlings in which improve, but will return when treatment is discontinued. serious disease and high mortalities can result. Transmission is primarily fecal-oral, but vertical Nutritional Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (NSHP) transmission is strongly suspected. Symptoms can be NSHP results from calcium deficiency caused by nonspecific such as lethargy, anorexia, stunted growth, either a lack of calcium in the diet or lack of UVB and failure to thrive, but it can also cause diarrhea and lighting resulting in hypovitaminosis D. Calcium is opisthotonus. Secon-dary bacterial and parasitic leached from the bones resulting in soft, bowed long infections are common. The virus typically causes an bones, fractures, deformities, stunted growth, enteritis and hepatitis, but can cause encephalitis as weakness, anorexia, and eventually tremors, seizures, well.1 Diagnosis is by PCR on cloacal swabs or fecal and death. History and exam findings are usually samples, or on biopsy or necropsy. There is no specific sufficient for diagnosis, but bloodwork or radiographs treatment for the virus. Treatment is aimed at providing can be helpful as well. Treatment involves oral and supportive care and treating any secondary infections. parenteral calcium, parenteral vitamin D and calcitonin Prognosis is usually guarded to poor. in some cases, along with correction of husbandry and supportive care. Prog-nosis depends on the severity of Internal Parasites signs at presentation. Recovery can take weeks to Internal parasites are extremely common in bearded months, and many patients will be left with permanent dragons. Oxyurids and flagellated protozoa are normal deformities. inhabitants of the bearded dragon GI tract, and coccidia (I. amphboluri) is endemic in the US population. All Obesity and Hepatic Lipidosis three are considered normal in small amounts, and if Captive bearded dragons are very commonly the bearded dragon is clinically normal then often no overfed, or fed insects high in fat; as such, obesity is treatment is needed. However, parasite overgrowth is very com-mon. Fat is stored in two fat bodies in the very common with concurrent stress and can cause coelomic cavity that are anchored caudally. Obesity illness. Oxyurids are the least likely to cause disease, can lead to other medical problems such as hepatic but in large numbers can cause anorexia, unthriftiness, lipidosis, dystocia, difficulty swallowing and breathing, impaction, and cloacitis.1 Treatment is with fenbenda- and diffi-culty moving. Dragons with hepatic lipidosis zole. The most common symptoms of protozoal often present with nonspecific signs such as anorexia enteritis are diarrhea, foul odor of the feces, blood in and lethargy, but are obviously overweight. Biochemis- the feces, and sometimes anorexia or regurgitation. tries can be normal, but can also demonstrate anemia, Treatment is with Metronidazole. Coccidiosis is leukocytosis, and elevated AST or bile acids. Adult especially common in young bearded dragons and dragons should be offered vegetable matter daily and often causes lethargy, anorexia, failure to thrive, insects only 1-2 times per week. Avoid high-fat insects, diarrhea, and blood in the stool. Ponazuril at 30mg/kg and also encourage time out of the cage for exercise. PO q48h for 3 doses is the current preferred Dragons with hepatic lipidosis often need syringe treatment.2 A novel visceral coccidian has also been feeding and supportive care for weeks to months, along reported to affect the kidneys.3 with hepatic support medications. Microsporidia Periodontal Disease Microsporidians are obligate intracellular parasites, Bearded dragons possess acrodont dentition in and can affect bearded dragons of all ages.1,4 It seems which the teeth are fused to the bone. The attachment to be very common in large breeding facilities, and is site is covered by a thin layer of epithelial rather than transmitted by the fecal-oral route and possibly gingival tissue, which predisposes to periodontal vertically as well. Signs include anorexia, lethargy, disease, particularly when fed soft foods. Clinical signs unthriftiness, swelling in affected areas, and acute range from staining to erythema and calculus death. Diagnosis is often made at necropsy. It can accumulation, to osteomyelitis, abscesses, bone loss, affect any body system, but has been reported in the and sepsis. Treat-ment involves radiographs, scaling of eyelids, joints, liver, kid-neys, intestine, and many the calculus, debridement of diseased bone, and others.1,4-6 Oral fenbendazole and topical fumagillin antibiotic use based on culture and sensitivity. The have been suggested as a treat-ment, but in most teeth can be brushed for prevention using a cotton- cases prognosis is poor.5 tipped applicator with dilute chlorhexidine solution. Yellow Fungus (CANV) Impaction and Constipation Fungal dermatitis is common in bearded dragons, Constipation is seen most often in adult dragons due and is often caused by the organism Chrysosporium to chronic dehydration, lack of exercise, and improper ana-morph of Nannizziopsis vriesii (CANV). This diet, but can also be seen in any age secondary to fungus typically causes raised, thickened, crusty substrate ingestion, NSHP, internal parasitism, lesions on the skin, and is also referred to as “yellow intestinal lesions (eg, masses), or renomegaly. fungus.” The fungus can also spread systemically. Squamous Aneurysms are a more recently described syndrome cell carcinomas in inland bearded dragons and usually arise from the internal carotid artery or (Pogona vitticeps). J Herp Med Surg 2011; 21: aorta.7 These usually present as a swelling on the head 101-6. or neck with no other symptoms, but affected dragons can also be pale, lethargic, and weak. Surgical removal is challenging; ultrasound or MRI can be used to determine the origin of the aneurysm so that it can be ligated and removed, but a blood donor should be available. Squamous Aneurysms are a more recently described syndrome cell carcinomas in inland bearded dragons and usually arise from the internal carotid artery or (Pogona vitticeps). J Herp Med Surg 2011; 21: aorta.7 These usually present as a swelling on the head 101-6. or neck with no other symptoms, but affected dragons can also be pale, lethargic, and weak. Surgical removal is challenging; ultrasound or MRI can be used to determine the origin of the aneurysm so that it can be ligated and removed, but a blood donor should be available.
